slides.title: Trust Me, I'm a Dev 👨‍💻
slides.content:
Sometimes you have information about a value's type that TypeScript possibly can't know.

For example, if you grab an HTML element by its ID: `document.getElementById('my-canvas')`.
TypeScript only knows this returns a generic `HTMLElement`. But YOU know for a fact it's specifically an `HTMLCanvasElement`.

slides.title: The `as` Keyword
slides.content: You can force TypeScript to treat a variable as a specific type using type assertions via the `as` keyword.

quizData.question: Does using `as` mathematically convert or cast the actual underlying JavaScript data at runtime?

options.text: Yes, it transforms the raw variable shape in memory.
No. Type assertions are strictly a compile-time concept. They vanish at runtime.
Yes, but only if the data is an Array.

quizData.explanation: TypeScript assertions exist entirely for the developer and the compiler. They have absolute zero impact or existence in the compiled final JavaScript runtime.

slides.title: The Danger of 'as' ⚠️
slides.content: Because `as` forces the compiler to blindly trust you, you can easily shoot yourself in the foot if you lie to it.

slides.title: Best Practice 💎
slides.content: Use `as` extremely sparingly. Only use it when you are interacting with third-party data or the DOM where TS structurally cannot infer the deep type on its own.
